input.content:
# Subjects discussed (running index)

Broad categories of topics covered across sessions, so past ground can be picked back up. Detail lives in the linked docs or per-project memory. Working style for all of these: [[userprofile.md]].

## OS / systems (core expertise)
- xv6 internals; Linux-inspired OS concepts. Interrupt handling, IDT/trap processing, timer interrupts, sleep/wakeup, futexes, scheduling, virtual memory.
- Recurring pattern: trace high-level API semantics down to kernel implementation; compare educational OS designs with production systems and find where simplifications break.

## Concurrency / synchronization (core expertise)
- Range locking, fine-grained vs hand-over-hand vs coarse locking, lock contention, work stealing, job dispatching, concurrent data structures.
- Themes: scalable designs, avoiding linear scans, partitioning work, preserving locality, complexity-vs-scalability tradeoffs; skeptical of textbook approaches under real workloads. → ThreePiecesSystem MEMORY.md

## Data structures (performance lens)
- Linked structures, trees, B-trees, heaps, indexing, interval/range management. Focus on why a structure exists, what gets faster/slower, behavior under insert/delete.

## Compilers / PLs / tooling internals
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, treesitter, vim plugin development, static analysis, code navigation. Strong interest in how tools work internally, wants to build vim/treesitter tooling. → [[vim.md]], [[languages.md]]
- nand2tetris stack (HDL → asm → VM → compiler → OS); Hack VM implemented.

## Embedded / hardware
- ESP32 + ESP-IDF toolchain, clangd/compile_commands, Xtensa vs system clang. → [[languages.md]], [[project-context]]
- WSL2 USB passthrough (usbipd-win) for flashing. → [[dotfile-env-setup]]
- Electronics: circuit design, hardware debugging, component analysis (TVS/Zener diodes, transistors, mercury switches), workstation/lab build. → [[aboutme.md]]

## Dev environment / infra
- Docker devcontainer topology, ephemeral vs persistent volumes, bare-repo dotfiles, declarative vs imperative provisioning, portability; vim → nvim migration. → [[dotfile-env-setup]], [[vim.md]]

## Claude Code / LLM tooling
- Claude Code extensibility (commands, skills, subagents, hooks, MCP). → [[claude.md]]
- Memory-system design (Documents/Secondary/Primary/Archive tiers, fetch-priority footnotes).
- RAG: chunk+embed+retrieve, dense+BM25 hybrid, embeddings, eval metrics (recall over precision); no built-in vector store in Claude Code → needs an MCP server. → [[projects.md]]

## Science / physics (curiosity)
- Electromagnetism, photons, quantum mechanics, engineering. Wants mechanistic explanations connecting intuition to formal models, not pop-sci. → [[aboutme.md]]

## XR / AR
- AR/XR glasses for mobile workstations (Viture, Xreal), display FOV technicalities.

input.content:
# Languages, toolchains & domain knowledge

Works across embedded/C/asm, Go, Python, JS/TS, shell. Builds tools from source; comfortable at the toolchain level. Depth varies by domain — see [[userprofile.md]] (deep in C/systems; learning Go/embedded/tooling).

## C / C++ (strongest, most-discussed language)
- Systems-level: memory management, performance, concurrency, compiler behavior/optimization effects. Default to deep treatment.
- Personal lib at `~/nand2tetris_volumn/myLib` exposed via `VERITY_LIBRARY_PATH` → `CPATH`/`LIBRARY_PATH`/`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` (see [[shell.md]]). `export_Clib` moves a lib into it + symlinks back.
- LSP via clangd (coc-clangd + clangd-lsp plugin). Lint via ALE gcc+clang, format clang-format.
- Debugging: gdb+pwndbg, valgrind/helgrind. Disasm: `asmcat`.
- **ESP32 / ESP-IDF v6.0.1**: clangd needs `compile_commands.json` from `idf.py -B build.clang reconfigure` (NO `-D IDF_TOOLCHAIN=clang` — system clang lacks the Xtensa backend). Activate IDF with `source ~/.espressif/tools/activate_idf_v6.0.1.sh`. Detail in per-project memory [[project-context]].

## Go (learning, for backend dev)
- `GOPATH=~/go`, `GOBIN=~/go/bin`, go at `/usr/local/go/bin`.
- vim: vim-go + coc-go; ftplugin/go.vim uses noexpandtab, vim-go text objects (af/if/ac/ic). Lint: golangci-lint.
- Interest: concurrency primitives, practical software construction, architecture decisions.

## Python
- `python=python3` alias; ipython as default REPL (`py`/`ipy`). `PYTHONBREAKPOINT` → IPython ipdb.
- Indent = 4 spaces (vim). LSP coc-pyright, lint flake8.
- Python 3.12.13 (a source tree also sits under esp32/ for IDF).

## JS / TS
- nvm-managed node. LSP coc-tsserver. Indent 2. (leetcodeChecklist is a node project.)

## Compilers / parsers / editor tooling (interest area)
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, **treesitter**, vim plugin development. Wants to build treesitter-based editing tools and vim extensions. → [[vim.md]], [[subjects.md]]

## CS foundation
- Completed **NAND2Tetris**; implemented the **Hack VM**. Frames questions from first principles, connecting abstractions to implementation across the full system stack. Learning-domain detail in [[subjects.md]].

# Projects

Most live under `~/nand2tetris_volumn/` (persistent /dev/sdc disk; survives container rebuilds). Root `/` is an ephemeral overlay — tooling installed there is lost on rebuild. Who the user is / how to collaborate: [[userprofile.md]].

## Active
- **ESP32 cat ventilation controller** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/projects/esp32/cat_vent/`) — ESP-IDF v6.0.1 firmware for the **cat-litterbox ventilation + filtration enclosure** (a physical maker build; the cat is 花生/Peanut — see [[aboutme.md]]). Current main.c is the IDF hello-world stub. Toolchain/clangd setup in [[project-context]] and [[languages.md]]. `project_setup.sh` must be **sourced** not executed.
- **Local RAG over textbooks** (planned, not built) — nomic-embed-text via Ollama + LanceDB + rank-bm25 hybrid search (60% dense / 40% BM25), OCR fallback (pytesseract / marker-pdf), exposed to Claude Code as an **MCP tool over stdio** inside the existing devcontainer. Textbooks bind-mounted read-only; rag_db as a named Docker volume. Ollama not yet installed in-container.
- **ThreePiecesSystem / OSTEP homework** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/ThreePiecesSystem/`) — concurrency exercises. syncList.c (custom RangeLock) + approximate counter done; hand-over-hand list and B-tree locking next. See that project's own MEMORY.md.

## Hardware / maker (physical, ongoing)
- **Electronics workstation / home research lab** — building out bench tooling (multimeters, oscilloscope), component-level work. Context in [[aboutme.md]].
- Fabrication: 3D modeling (Fusion 360/FreeCAD), laser engraving, acrylic joining, woodworking. The cat_vent enclosure is the firmware↔fabrication crossover.

## Dev-environment / infra (cross-cutting)
- Containerization, dotfile management, env portability — the big ongoing meta-project. Architecture, runtime topology, migration goals in per-project memory [[dotfile-env-setup]]. Headline goals: vim→nvim, per-project containers, Dockerfile-based portability, bake ESP-IDF into an image instead of the ephemeral layer.

## Other / dormant
- `leetcodeChecklist` — node/JS project (leetcode tracking).
- `syncReaction` — youtube-bookmarker / picture-in-picture experiments.
- `champion_contest_scrapper.py` — scraper script.

# Languages, toolchains & domain knowledge

Works across embedded/C/asm, Go, Python, JS/TS, shell. Builds tools from source; comfortable at the toolchain level. Depth varies by domain — see [[userprofile.md]] (deep in C/systems; learning Go/embedded/tooling).

## C / C++ (strongest, most-discussed language)
- Systems-level: memory management, performance, concurrency, compiler behavior/optimization effects. Default to deep treatment.
- Personal lib at `~/nand2tetris_volumn/myLib` exposed via `VERITY_LIBRARY_PATH` → `CPATH`/`LIBRARY_PATH`/`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` (see [[shell.md]]). `export_Clib` moves a lib into it + symlinks back.
- LSP via clangd (coc-clangd + clangd-lsp plugin). Lint via ALE gcc+clang, format clang-format.
- Debugging: gdb+pwndbg, valgrind/helgrind. Disasm: `asmcat`.
- **ESP32 / ESP-IDF v6.0.1**: clangd needs `compile_commands.json` from `idf.py -B build.clang reconfigure` (NO `-D IDF_TOOLCHAIN=clang` — system clang lacks the Xtensa backend). Activate IDF with `source ~/.espressif/tools/activate_idf_v6.0.1.sh`. Detail in per-project memory [[project-context]].

## Go (learning, for backend dev)
- `GOPATH=~/go`, `GOBIN=~/go/bin`, go at `/usr/local/go/bin`.
- vim: vim-go + coc-go; ftplugin/go.vim uses noexpandtab, vim-go text objects (af/if/ac/ic). Lint: golangci-lint.
- Interest: concurrency primitives, practical software construction, architecture decisions.

## Python
- `python=python3` alias; ipython as default REPL (`py`/`ipy`). `PYTHONBREAKPOINT` → IPython ipdb.
- Indent = 4 spaces (vim). LSP coc-pyright, lint flake8.
- Python 3.12.13 (a source tree also sits under esp32/ for IDF).

## JS / TS
- nvm-managed node. LSP coc-tsserver. Indent 2. (leetcodeChecklist is a node project.)

## Compilers / parsers / editor tooling (interest area)
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, **treesitter**, vim plugin development. Wants to build treesitter-based editing tools and vim extensions. → [[vim.md]], [[subjects.md]]

## CS foundation
- Completed **NAND2Tetris**; implemented the **Hack VM**. Frames questions from first principles, connecting abstractions to implementation across the full system stack. Learning-domain detail in [[subjects.md]].

# Projects

Most live under `~/nand2tetris_volumn/` (persistent /dev/sdc disk; survives container rebuilds). Root `/` is an ephemeral overlay — tooling installed there is lost on rebuild. Who the user is / how to collaborate: [[userprofile.md]].

## Active
- **ESP32 cat ventilation controller** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/projects/esp32/cat_vent/`) — ESP-IDF v6.0.1 firmware for the **cat-litterbox ventilation + filtration enclosure** (a physical maker build; the cat is 花生/Peanut — see [[aboutme.md]]). Current main.c is the IDF hello-world stub. Toolchain/clangd setup in [[project-context]] and [[languages.md]]. `project_setup.sh` must be **sourced** not executed.
- **Local RAG over textbooks** (planned, not built) — nomic-embed-text via Ollama + LanceDB + rank-bm25 hybrid search (60% dense / 40% BM25), OCR fallback (pytesseract / marker-pdf), exposed to Claude Code as an **MCP tool over stdio** inside the existing devcontainer. Textbooks bind-mounted read-only; rag_db as a named Docker volume. Ollama not yet installed in-container.
- **ThreePiecesSystem / OSTEP homework** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/ThreePiecesSystem/`) — concurrency exercises. syncList.c (custom RangeLock) + approximate counter done; hand-over-hand list and B-tree locking next. See that project's own MEMORY.md.

## Hardware / maker (physical, ongoing)
- **Electronics workstation / home research lab** — building out bench tooling (multimeters, oscilloscope), component-level work. Context in [[aboutme.md]].
- Fabrication: 3D modeling (Fusion 360/FreeCAD), laser engraving, acrylic joining, woodworking. The cat_vent enclosure is the firmware↔fabrication crossover.

## Dev-environment / infra (cross-cutting)
- Containerization, dotfile management, env portability — the big ongoing meta-project. Architecture, runtime topology, migration goals in per-project memory [[dotfile-env-setup]]. Headline goals: vim→nvim, per-project containers, Dockerfile-based portability, bake ESP-IDF into an image instead of the ephemeral layer.

## Other / dormant
- `leetcodeChecklist` — node/JS project (leetcode tracking).
- `syncReaction` — youtube-bookmarker / picture-in-picture experiments.
- `champion_contest_scrapper.py` — scraper script.
- `~/LLM/` — openwebui_pipline, nginx_serve, vim_wrapper (LLM-serving experiments).
- Planned tooling: treesitter-based editing tools, vim extensions (see [[languages.md]]).

## Claude Code memory system itself
Designing the global memory tiers (Documents/Secondary/Primary/Archive) — see [[claude.md]] and todo.

## Contexts
- this is claude code in Ubuntu Linux
- the system is for general programming related personal project development.
- the system is running in a docker container on windows host, main tools - zsh/tmux/vim.

## Rules
- big picture breakdown for unfamiliar subject, and detailed explanation for complicated ones. don't assume prior knowledge unless you know I know.
- expand on the topic if you see value in me learning it more - what, why, where, how, when to learn it.
- details on complex or domain-specific topics needs to be verified and referenced.
- the x-y question rules: infer the intend of my question to decide if clarify the problem at hand is better to solve the problem. but sometimes I'm just qurious about things, so let it slides.
- refer to [Documents](##Documents) if the description fits the use cases.
- refer to [Archive](##Archive) if the resource name fits the use cases. 
- Batch related file reads into single operations.

## Reference rules
- markdown footnotes [^note] in this CLAUDE.md file is for marking the rank of a linked document:
- [^0] fetch only if explicitly asked
- [^1] fetch only if this specific file is clearly relevant
- [^2] fetch when description matches the topic at hand, higher number takes priority if both fits.
- [^3, 4, 5...] fetch when general area matches; higher number takes priority if both fits.
- at the start of each task/topic, scan all [^1+] file descriptions; fetch if they match.
- when about to advise on a topic and uncertain of my context for it, scan all [^1+] file descriptions; fetch if they match.

## Documents
> under directory ~/.claude/memory_docs_live/
- [broad category of previously discussed subjects](subjects.md) [^3]
- [specific projects I am/was working on](projects.md) [^3]
- [specific tools related stuff](tools.md) [^2]
- [specific setups and preferences on vim](vim.md) [^2]
- [specific setups and preferences on shell script](shell.md) [^2]
- [specific setups and preferences on claude code](claude.md) [^2]
- [specific setups, toolchains, and knowledge for languages](languages.md) [^2]
- [overall todo list](todo.md) [^2]
- [about me - don't read for any serious works, it's for casual conversation](aboutme.md) [^1]

## Archive 
> under directory ~/.claude/memory_docs_archive/
> archive for static documents, follow the link if cataloged resources might be of help
- default [^0]
```json
```

## Secondary Memories
> under directory ~/.claude/memory_live/
> a json dict for description to filepath mappings
- default [^4]
```json
{}
```

## Primary Memories
> always-loaded, importnat memories of any kind (context/rule/fact/task) worth carrying across sessions — even if only briefly important.
> a json list of [memory_string, attributes] tuples. attributes schema (standardized for automation):
> - `type`: "context" | "rule" | "fact" | "task" — kind of memory
> - `created`: "YYYY-MM-DD" — when pinned
> - `expires`: "YYYY-MM-DD" | null — demote/prune after this date; null = persistent
> - `priority`: 1-5 — higher = kept longer / wins pruning ties
> - `tags`: [string] — for grouping/retrieval
```json

  ["Next planned project: local RAG-over-textbooks (Ollama nomic-embed-text + LanceDB + rank-bm25 hybrid 60/40, OCR fallback) exposed as an MCP stdio tool inside the existing devcontainer. Don't suggest installing/saving to the ephemeral layer without flagging persistence. Detail: memory_live/env-runtime-topology.md", {"type": "rule", "created": "2026-06-01", "expires": null, "priority": 5, "tags": ["environment", "container", "constraint"]}]
]
```

Done. The two sessions are merged and distributed.

## What went where

| Memory | Added |
|---|---|
| **`userprofile.md`** (new Document, `[^3]`) | The full working profile — depth-by-domain, "show real values & state transitions," the **missing-bridge** insight, mechanisms-over-memorization, accuracy/sources/uncertainty contract |
| **`aboutme.md`** (casual `[^1]`) | Name (Lin Wu An), Hualien/Taiwan, languages, maker/DIY, electronics lab, coffee, hiking, birding, Genshin, sketching, physics curiosity, XR; Peanut |
| **`subjects.md`** | New domain sections: OS/xv6, concurrency, data structures, compilers/treesitter/LSP, electronics/hardware, physics, XR/AR |
| **`languages.md`** | C marked strongest; Go-for-backend; treesitter/parser/LSP interest; NAND2Tetris + Hack VM |
| **`projects.md`** | Electronics workstation/lab; cat_vent↔litterbox↔Peanut crossover |
| **Primary Memory** | One always-loaded rule: deep version for core systems, scaffold for newer areas |

## The one thing I want to flag

The merged profile creates a **real tension with your CLAUDE.md Rules** (line 7):

> *"don't assume prior knowledge unless you know I know"* + the `aboutme` "amateur" self-label

…vs the converged profile's *"don't give beginner framing for systems/concurrency/OS/compilers."* Both are true — the resolution is **domain-dependent**, which I wrote into both `userprofile.md` (the "Reconciling with CLAUDE.md" section) and the Primary entry. But your **Rules line 7 itself still reads beginner-default**. You may want to tweak it to something like *"assume strong prior knowledge in core systems/CS; scaffold in newer areas (Go, embedded, tooling, infra)."* I didn't touch the Rules since you author those by hand — your call.

Two small things I noticed in your edits (not fixing without your say-so): line 52 now reads "importnat" (typo), and Archive's JSON block on line 41 is still empty (no `{}`) — will fail a parser when the automation reads it.

content.text:
What you're describing is actually a *third* thing, distinct from both poles — and it has a name worth knowing because it gives a crisp target.

## The concept: scaffold at the edge (Zone of Proximal Development)

Vygotsky's idea: learning is most productive in the band **just beyond what you can already do on your own** — not stuff you've mastered (boring, slow), not stuff too far ahead (no foothold). "One or two steps ahead of current knowledge" is almost exactly the textbook definition of the ZPD. Naming it matters because it turns a vague preference into an operational rule: *find the current level, then aim at the edge.*

This reframes what I wrote. The depth-by-domain split (deep for systems / scaffold for new) isn't a separate rule from your learning strategy — it's the **input** to a single strategy:

- The domain priors tell me **where your current level is** (high in systems/OS/C/compilers; lower in Go/embedded/tooling/infra).
- The ZPD rule says **what to do with that**: pitch a step or two past it, in *every* domain.

So "go deep in systems" and "scaffold in Go" stop being two contradictory rules — they're the same rule (aim at the edge) applied to two different starting points. For systems, the edge is genuinely advanced/mechanism-level. For embedded, the edge is intro-but-not-condescending. That's why my earlier "no beginner framing vs. scaffold more" binary felt off — it was treating the *input* as if it were the *rule*.

## What I'd modify

**1. Rules line 7** — replace the "don't assume prior knowledge" clause:

> *"for unfamiliar subjects give the big-picture breakdown, for complicated ones go detailed. calibrate to my current level then aim a step or two past it — bring me up to speed, neither beginner-default nor assume-mastery. gauge level from domain priors (see userprofile.md); when unsure, probe rather than guess."*

**2. The Primary entry + `userprofile.md`** — swap the binary ("no beginner framing / scaffold more") for the calibration framing: domain priors = where the edge is, ZPD = aim just past it. Add the "when unsure of level, probe" bit — that's the safety valve that makes it work without me guessing wrong.

**3. One genuinely useful addition** — "probe when unsure." The failure mode of ZPD-targeting is *misjudging* your level. A cheap fix: when I can't tell, drop a quick calibration check ("you've worked with X before?") rather than committing to a depth blind. Worth encoding as a rule.

Want me to apply all three? Line 7 is hand-authored by you, so I'll do that one only if you confirm the wording; I can do the `userprofile.md` / Primary reframe right away since those are the auto-managed tier.

And you're right that any rule pointing at `userprofile.md` is fragile until automation keeps that file honest.

Let me read the current file (you've been editing it) and apply.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# User working profile (how to collaborate)

> Highest-value memory for technical sessions. Distilled from cross-session summaries where multiple assistants independently converged on the same picture.

## Technical depth — domain-dependent (important)
Genuine, deep expertise in: **systems programming, operating systems, concurrency/synchronization, data structures, C, compilers & programming languages, low-level software design.** For these, default to the *deep version* — precise terminology, internal implementation details, complexity analysis, real-world constraints, historical context. Do NOT simplify or use beginner framing unless asked. If a topic touches systems/concurrency/OS/compilers/low-level, assume he wants the deeper version.

Newer / actively learning: **Go (for backend dev), embedded / ESP-IDF toolchains, editor-tooling internals (treesitter, LSP, plugin dev), LLM/RAG tooling, dev-infra (Docker/containers).** Here more scaffolding helps — but still mechanism-first, not hand-holding.

Background: completed **NAND2Tetris**, implemented the **Hack VM**. Learns by *building systems from first principles*; dissatisfied when things are left as black boxes. Linux/CLI-driven (vim, docker, sed, awk, fzf).

## How to explain
- **Concrete over abstract.** Show a small example with *real values*, the actual state transitions, and what each operation costs. Abstract-only descriptions are considered unhelpful.
- **Supply the missing bridge.** His recurring need is not "more detail" — it's the omitted intermediate step (give A→B→C, not A→C). He's unusually good at detecting skipped steps and asks follow-ups to surface them. Structure explanations to include the bridge.
- **Mechanisms over memorization.** Always cover "why does this exist / how does it actually work underneath / what are the tradeoffs / what alternatives exist."
- **Order:** start with the direct answer, then concrete example, then tradeoffs, then implementation detail.

## Communication contract
- Accuracy over confidence. State uncertainty explicitly. Don't invent or guess.
- Cite sources for factual claims.
- Flag XY problems and offer better framings.
- Concise is good — but never at the expense of technical clarity.
- Curiosity is a valid motivation; not every question is goal-directed (matches the x-y "let it slide" rule).

## Reconciling with CLAUDE.md
The casual self-label "amateur, may lack fundamentals" ([[aboutme.md]]) is modesty about *peripheral* areas, not an instruction to simplify core-systems topics. Resolve the Rules' "don't assume prior knowledge unless you know I know" by domain: assume strong prior knowledge across the core-systems list above; scaffold in the newer areas.

Do NOT simplify or use beginner framing unless asked. If a topic touches systems/concurrency/OS/compilers/low-level, assume he wants the deeper version.

content.text:
All applied. Summary of the changes:

**Deleted** — the Primary Memory entry that referenced `userprofile.md` for depth calibration. Behavior no longer depends on a file you can't guarantee is current pre-automation.

**Rewrote Rules lines 7–8** to encode your actual bias:
- *"default to direct, complete answers at full implementation depth — don't dumb down or slow the implementation to fit my level… I'll ask when I feel lost; trust that over pre-emptive hand-holding."*
- *"expand… as an addition alongside the direct answer, not a detour that delays the implementation."*

The key shift: learning is **pull, not push**. Full depth is the default; you self-pace by asking; teaching-expansion rides *alongside* the answer instead of throttling it.

**Aligned `userprofile.md`** so the newer-domains line says "thinner knowledge ≠ slow down / hand-hold" instead of "more scaffolding helps."

---

Why this resolves the original tension cleanly: the problem was never beginner-vs-expert. It was **push-vs-pull**. A "calibrate to level" rule (either direction) makes *me* guess your level and gate the answer on it — friction when you just want the implementation. Your model removes the guessing: go full-depth, you reach in when you want more. The domain info in `userprofile.md` survives as *context* (where you're strong) but stops being a *throttle*.

One still-open item from earlier, your call whenever: Archive's JSON block (line ~41) is still an empty fenced block with no `{}` — it'll throw when the automation parses it.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is designing and populating a **tiered global memory system** for Claude Code, BEFORE implementing a planned RAG project. Explicit intents in sequence:
   - Evaluate the structure of their memory system (CLAUDE.md + memory dirs) against humanlayer's "writing a good CLAUDE.md" guidelines, before proceeding to RAG.
   - Understand/refine the 4-tier memory design and its fetch-priority footnote rules.
   - Populate the global memory files: **Documents** under `~/.claude/memory_docs_live/` and **Secondary Memories** under `~/.claude/memory_live/`, by reading all available Claude Code memories, project files, and system configs.
   - Design the **Primary Memories** inline JSON (list of `[memory_string, attributes_dict]` tuples) with a `>`-quote schema template.
   - Combine two external LLM-session summaries (Gemini + ChatGPT) and distribute them into appropriate memory files.
   - MOST RECENT: Remove the Primary entry that references userprofile.md (don't make behavior depend on a profile file that can't be guaranteed current until automation is built), and change the rules to reflect the user's actual bias: **default to direct, full-implementation-depth answers that don't sacrifice implementation to fit the user's level; the user asks when they feel lost.**

   The user's stated future task (NOT yet started): build a local RAG-over-textbooks system (Ollama nomic-embed-text + LanceDB + rank-bm25 hybrid 60/40 + OCR fallback) exposed as an MCP stdio tool inside the existing devcontainer.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Claude Code 4-tier global memory system with distinct auto-management strategies:
     - **Documents** (`~/.claude/memory_docs_live/`): manually-managed reference list, auto-managed file content. Footnotes [^2-3].
     - **Archive** (`~/.claude/memory_docs_archive/`): semi-auto reference, static file content. [^0].
     - **Secondary Memories** (`~/.claude/memory_live/`): auto-managed description→filepath JSON + auto-managed files. [^4].
     - **Primary Memories** (inline JSON in CLAUDE.md): always-loaded, importance equal to the Rules/Contexts bullets, any kind (context/rule/fact/task), can be briefly-important pinned working set.
   - Footnote fetch-priority system: `[^n]` where **higher number = higher fetch priority** (user corrected me on this). [^0]=only if asked, [^1]=specific file clearly relevant, [^2]=description matches topic, [^3+]=general area matches.
   - Fetch triggers: scan [^1+] descriptions at task/topic start; in-session re-fetch blocked by harness when file unchanged ("Wasted call" reminder).
   - Progressive disclosure (lean CLAUDE.md + referenced detail files) — humanlayer guideline (under 60 lines ideal, under 300 max; LLMs follow ~150-200 instructions reliably).
   - `[[wiki-link]]` cross-references between memory files.
   - Per-project auto-memory (`~/.claude/projects/<slug>/memory/`) is SEPARATE from the global tiers and only loads in that project's dir.

file.content:
# Projects

Most live under `~/nand2tetris_volumn/` (persistent /dev/sdc disk; survives container rebuilds). Root `/` is an ephemeral overlay — tooling installed there is lost on rebuild. Who the user is / how to collaborate: [[userprofile.md]].

## Active
- **ESP32 cat ventilation controller** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/projects/esp32/cat_vent/`) — ESP-IDF v6.0.1 firmware for the **cat-litterbox ventilation + filtration enclosure** (a physical maker build; the cat is 花生/Peanut — see [[aboutme.md]]). Current main.c is the IDF hello-world stub. Toolchain/clangd setup in [[project-context]] and [[languages.md]]. `project_setup.sh` must be **sourced** not executed.
- **Local RAG over textbooks** (planned, not built) — nomic-embed-text via Ollama + LanceDB + rank-bm25 hybrid search (60% dense / 40% BM25), OCR fallback (pytesseract / marker-pdf), exposed to Claude Code as an **MCP tool over stdio** inside the existing devcontainer. Textbooks bind-mounted read-only; rag_db as a named Docker volume. Ollama not yet installed in-container.
- **ThreePiecesSystem / OSTEP homework** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/ThreePiecesSystem/`) — concurrency exercises. syncList.c (custom RangeLock) + approximate counter done; hand-over-hand list and B-tree locking next. See that project's own MEMORY.md.

## Hardware / maker (physical, ongoing)
- **Electronics workstation / home research lab** — building out bench tooling (multimeters, oscilloscope), component-level work. Context in [[aboutme.md]].
- Fabrication: 3D modeling (Fusion 360/FreeCAD), laser engraving, acrylic joining, woodworking. The cat_vent enclosure is the firmware↔fabrication crossover.

## Dev-environment / infra (cross-cutting)
- Containerization, dotfile management, env portability — the big ongoing meta-project. Architecture, runtime topology, migration goals in per-project memory [[dotfile-env-setup]]. Headline goals: vim→nvim, per-project containers, Dockerfile-based portability, bake ESP-IDF into an image instead of the ephemeral layer.

## Other / dormant
- `leetcodeChecklist` — node/JS project (leetcode tracking).
- `syncReaction` — youtube-bookmarker / picture-in-picture experiments.
- `champion_contest_scrapper.py` — scraper script.
- `~/LLM/` — openwebui_pipline, nginx_serve, vim_wrapper (LLM-serving experiments).
- Planned tooling: treesitter-based editing tools, vim extensions (see [[languages.md]]).

## Claude Code memory system itself
Designing the global memory tiers (Documents/Secondary/Primary/Archive) — see [[claude.md]] and todo.

# Languages, toolchains & domain knowledge

Works across embedded/C/asm, Go, Python, JS/TS, shell. Builds tools from source; comfortable at the toolchain level. Depth varies by domain — see [[userprofile.md]] (deep in C/systems; learning Go/embedded/tooling).

## C / C++ (strongest, most-discussed language)
- Systems-level: memory management, performance, concurrency, compiler behavior/optimization effects. Default to deep treatment.
- Personal lib at `~/nand2tetris_volumn/myLib` exposed via `VERITY_LIBRARY_PATH` → `CPATH`/`LIBRARY_PATH`/`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` (see [[shell.md]]). `export_Clib` moves a lib into it + symlinks back.
- LSP via clangd (coc-clangd + clangd-lsp plugin). Lint via ALE gcc+clang, format clang-format.
- Debugging: gdb+pwndbg, valgrind/helgrind. Disasm: `asmcat`.
- **ESP32 / ESP-IDF v6.0.1**: clangd needs `compile_commands.json` from `idf.py -B build.clang reconfigure` (NO `-D IDF_TOOLCHAIN=clang` — system clang lacks the Xtensa backend). Activate IDF with `source ~/.espressif/tools/activate_idf_v6.0.1.sh`. Detail in per-project memory [[project-context]].

## Go (learning, for backend dev)
- `GOPATH=~/go`, `GOBIN=~/go/bin`, go at `/usr/local/go/bin`.
- vim: vim-go + coc-go; ftplugin/go.vim uses noexpandtab, vim-go text objects (af/if/ac/ic). Lint: golangci-lint.
- Interest: concurrency primitives, practical software construction, architecture decisions.

## Python
- `python=python3` alias; ipython as default REPL (`py`/`ipy`). `PYTHONBREAKPOINT` → IPython ipdb.
- Indent = 4 spaces (vim). LSP coc-pyright, lint flake8.
- Python 3.12.13 (a source tree also sits under esp32/ for IDF).

## JS / TS
- nvm-managed node. LSP coc-tsserver. Indent 2. (leetcodeChecklist is a node project.)

## Compilers / parsers / editor tooling (interest area)
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, **treesitter**, vim plugin development. Wants to build treesitter-based editing tools and vim extensions. → [[vim.md]], [[subjects.md]]

## CS foundation
- Completed **NAND2Tetris**; implemented the **Hack VM**. Frames questions from first principles, connecting abstractions to implementation across the full system stack. Learning-domain detail in [[subjects.md]].

# Subjects discussed (running index)

Broad categories of topics covered across sessions, so past ground can be picked back up. Detail lives in the linked docs or per-project memory. Working style for all of these: [[userprofile.md]].

## OS / systems (core expertise)
- xv6 internals; Linux-inspired OS concepts. Interrupt handling, IDT/trap processing, timer interrupts, sleep/wakeup, futexes, scheduling, virtual memory.
- Recurring pattern: trace high-level API semantics down to kernel implementation; compare educational OS designs with production systems and find where simplifications break.

## Concurrency / synchronization (core expertise)
- Range locking, fine-grained vs hand-over-hand vs coarse locking, lock contention, work stealing, job dispatching, concurrent data structures.
- Themes: scalable designs, avoiding linear scans, partitioning work, preserving locality, complexity-vs-scalability tradeoffs; skeptical of textbook approaches under real workloads. → ThreePiecesSystem MEMORY.md

## Data structures (performance lens)
- Linked structures, trees, B-trees, heaps, indexing, interval/range management. Focus on why a structure exists, what gets faster/slower, behavior under insert/delete.

## Compilers / PLs / tooling internals
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, treesitter, vim plugin development, static analysis, code navigation. Strong interest in how tools work internally, wants to build vim/treesitter tooling. → [[vim.md]], [[languages.md]]
- nand2tetris stack (HDL → asm → VM → compiler → OS); Hack VM implemented.

## Embedded / hardware
- ESP32 + ESP-IDF toolchain, clangd/compile_commands, Xtensa vs system clang. → [[languages.md]], [[project-context]]
- WSL2 USB passthrough (usbipd-win) for flashing. → [[dotfile-env-setup]]
- Electronics: circuit design, hardware debugging, component analysis (TVS/Zener diodes, transistors, mercury switches), workstation/lab build. → [[aboutme.md]]

## Dev environment / infra
- Docker devcontainer topology, ephemeral vs persistent volumes, bare-repo dotfiles, declarative vs imperative provisioning, portability; vim → nvim migration. → [[dotfile-env-setup]], [[vim.md]]

## Claude Code / LLM tooling
- Claude Code extensibility (commands, skills, subagents, hooks, MCP). → [[claude.md]]
- Memory-system design (Documents/Secondary/Primary/Archive tiers, fetch-priority footnotes).
- RAG: chunk+embed+retrieve, dense+BM25 hybrid, embeddings, eval metrics (recall over precision); no built-in vector store in Claude Code → needs an MCP server. → [[projects.md]]

## Science / physics (curiosity)
- Electromagnetism, photons, quantum mechanics, engineering. Wants mechanistic explanations connecting intuition to formal models, not pop-sci. → [[aboutme.md]]

## XR / AR
- AR/XR glasses for mobile workstations (Viture, Xreal), display FOV technicalities.

# About me (casual — not for serious technical work)
**Based in Taiwan**. Speaks Mandarin and English.
amateur, learning as I go, may lack fundamentals in some unexpected areas so watchout.

## Maker / DIY
- 3D modeling (Fusion 360, FreeCAD), laser engraving, acrylic joining, sewing, woodworking (e.g. slotted wooden wall).
- Building a dedicated **electronics workstation / home research lab** — multimeters, oscilloscope; analyzes components (TVS/Zener diodes, transistors like 2N5550/SS8050, mercury switches).
- Practical builds, notably a **cat-litterbox ventilation + filtration enclosure** — the same system the esp32 **cat_vent** firmware controls (see [[projects.md]]).

## Interest
- **Gaming:** Minecraft (technical minecraft), Genshin Impact (mechanics, character builds), League of Legends (Esports), Valorant (Esports).
- **learning to draw
- **Physics: major in but not graduated from.

## Pets
- Cat named **花生 (Peanut)**, shared with family. The cat_vent project exists for Peanut's litterbox.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Short answer: the thing throttling reads isn't file permissions — I just read four of them fine. It's your **footnote gating**. "Free to read" means deciding which of two layers a file lives in.

## The two layers (this is the crux)

Your memory has an **index** and **bodies**, and they load by totally different mechanisms:

| | What it is | How it loads |
|---|---|---|
| **Index** | the Documents bullet lines + the Primary JSON, *inside* CLAUDE.md | **always loaded**, every session, unconditionally — it's part of CLAUDE.md itself |
| **Bodies** | the actual `vim.md`, `languages.md`, … file contents | **on-demand**, gated by your `[^n]` footnotes + the Reference rules |

So a file is never truly "blocked" — its *description* is always in front of me via the index. The footnote only decides how eagerly I pull the **body**. To "set a file free" you pick how free, and there are three levers, weakest → strongest:

## Lever 1 — soften the Reference-rules wording (affects all files)
Right now the rules read as a throttle ("fetch *only if* clearly relevant"). That biases me toward *not* reading. If you want the default to lean toward reading, rephrase to "when in doubt, fetch" for the [^2+] tier. This changes my disposition globally without touching any footnote.

## Lever 2 — bump a file's footnote (affects one file)
Since higher number = higher fetch priority in your scheme, moving `languages.md` from `[^2]` to `[^4]` means I grab it on a *general-area* match, not just an exact-topic match. This is the per-file dial. It's still **advisory** — I obey footnotes because they're instructions, not because the harness enforces them.

## Lever 3 — native `@import` (mechanical, guaranteed always-loaded)
Claude Code's memory system supports `@import` syntax: put a line like

```
@/root/.claude/memory_docs_live/languages.md
```

in CLAUDE.md, and the **harness itself splices that file's body into context** every session — bypassing your footnote system entirely (recursive, up to ~5 hops deep). This is the only *mechanical* "always read," vs. the footnotes which are *advisory* prompting. Cost: it's permanent context weight, so reserve it for files you genuinely want in every single session (it defeats the whole progressive-disclosure point if you `@import` everything).

---

**Which one you want depends on the goal:**
- "I want you to *hesitate less*" → Lever 1 (reword the rule).
- "*This specific file* matters more than its footnote says" → Lever 2 (bump it).
- "*This file* must be in context unconditionally, no judgment call" → Lever 3 (`@import`, and drop it from the gated Documents list so it isn't double-counted).

The footnote system was *built* to keep bodies out of context until needed — so before loosening, the question worth asking is which files actually hurt from being read late. My guess: none of them do today, because the index already tells me what's in each. That's the argument for leaving the footnotes conservative and only `@import`-ing a file once you catch yourself repeatedly wishing it had been auto-loaded.

Want me to apply any of these — e.g. reword the Reference rules to lean-toward-reading, or `@import` a specific file?

message.content: tell me
does one session reload a already read file if it has be modified since?

content.text:
No automatic reload — there's no file-watcher. If a file changes mid-session, you need to explicitly tell me to re-read it.

Two behaviors to know:

- **File changed since last read** → `Read` succeeds and returns fresh content.
- **File unchanged since last read** → harness blocks it with a "Wasted call" warning and returns the cached version. This is what you'd hit if you asked me to re-read something I already have.

For `@import`-ed files: they're spliced in once at session start. Mid-session edits to them won't be picked up until the next session (or an explicit `Read` call from you).

message.content: can't I just change the file read into the context in the session?

content.text:
Yes — if you edit the file on disk, the next `Read` goes through clean. The "Wasted call" block only fires when the file is **unchanged**; a modified file always re-reads fine.

So mid-session the flow is just: edit the file → tell me to re-read it → updated content is in context.

message.content: no, I mean... will re-reading basically dupe the file again into the context? or you're doing something more clever like diff versioning?

content.text:
Dupe. Every `Read` appends the full file content as a new tool result in the conversation — no diffing, no replacing the old copy. If I read `languages.md` at the start and again after you edited it, both full copies are in the context window.

In practice this is rarely a problem since your memory files are small. But it's worth knowing if you're trying to keep context lean.

message.content: why not just replace the original read file? aren't LLM stateless and context can be freely modified?

lastPrompt: why not just replace the original read file? aren't LLM stateless and context can be freely modified?

aiTitle: Generate template folders without external dependencies

content.text:
The context isn't freely modifiable mid-conversation at the **API level**. The Anthropic Messages API takes an immutable ordered list of messages — there's no "replace message N" operation. Each tool result is a fixed message in that list.

To "replace" an old read you'd have to rebuild the entire message array from scratch and re-send it, which would also bust the prompt cache (expensive).

The only thing Claude Code does that's conceptually close is `/compact` — it summarizes the whole conversation into a single message and drops the history. That's destructive compression, not surgical replacement.

So it's not a clever design choice — it's a constraint of how the underlying API works. The context is append-only.

## Contexts
- this is claude code in Ubuntu Linux
- the system is for general programming related personal project development.
- the system is running in a docker container on windows host, main tools - zsh/tmux/vim.

## Rules
- default to direct, complete answers at full implementation depth — don't dumb down or slow the implementation to fit my level. big-picture breakdown for unfamiliar subjects, detailed for complicated ones. I'll ask when I feel lost; trust that over pre-emptive hand-holding.
- expand on a topic when there's genuine learning value (what/why/where/how/when) — as an addition alongside the direct answer, not a detour that delays the implementation.
- details on complex or domain-specific topics needs to be verified and referenced.
- the x-y question rules: infer the intend of my question to decide if clarify the problem at hand is better to solve the problem. but sometimes I'm just qurious about things, so let it slides.
- refer to [Documents](##Documents) if the description fits the use cases.
- refer to [Archive](##Archive) if the resource name fits the use cases. 
- Batch related file reads into single operations.

## Reference rules
- markdown footnotes [^note] in this CLAUDE.md file is for marking the rank of a linked document:
- [^0] fetch only if explicitly asked
- [^1] fetch only if this specific file is clearly relevant
- [^2] fetch when description matches the topic at hand, higher number takes priority if both fits.
- [^3, 4, 5...] fetch when general area matches; higher number takes priority if both fits.
- at the start of each task/topic, scan all [^1+] file descriptions; fetch if they match.
- when about to advise on a topic and uncertain of my context for it, scan all [^1+] file descriptions; fetch if they match.

## Documents
> under directory ~/.claude/memory_docs_live/
- [broad category of previously discussed subjects](subjects.md) [^3]
- [specific projects I am/was working on](projects.md) [^3]

## Archive

```json

```

## Secondary Memories

## Primary Memories
> always-loaded, important memories of any kind (context/rule/fact/task) worth carrying across sessions — even if only briefly important.
> a json list of [memory_string, attributes] tuples. attributes schema (standardized for automation):
> - `type`: "context" | "rule" | "fact" | "task" — kind of memory
> - `created`: "YYYY-MM-DD" — when pinned
> - `expires`: "YYYY-MM-DD" | null — demote/prune after this date; null = persistent
> - `priority`: 1-5 — higher = kept longer / wins pruning ties
> - `tags`: [string] — for grouping/retrieval

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Claude Code permission system**: file read permissions are granted via `~/.claude/settings.json` under `permissions.allow` as patterns like `"Read(~/.claude/memory_docs_live/**)"` — this prevents per-call permission prompts for every session.
   - **Context is append-only**: The Anthropic Messages API takes an immutable ordered list of messages. There is no "replace message N" operation; every `Read` tool call appends a new tool result into the context. Re-reading a file duplicates its full content — no diffing or versioning.
   - **"Wasted call" block**: The harness blocks re-reading a file that is unchanged since the last read. If the file has been modified on disk, the re-read goes through cleanly and appends fresh content.
   - **No automatic file reload**: There is no file-watcher. Edited files must be explicitly re-read.
   - **@import**: Splices a file body into context at session start only — not real-time; does not auto-reload on edit.
   - **Footnote gating (clarified)**: The `[^n]` system in CLAUDE.md is advisory — higher number = higher fetch priority. It controls when I pull a file body, not whether the file is accessible. It is NOT a file permission mechanism.

file.content:
# About me (casual — not for serious technical work)
**Based in Taiwan**. Speaks Mandarin and English.
amateur, learning as I go, may lack fundamentals in some unexpected areas so watchout.

## Maker / DIY
- 3D modeling (Fusion 360, FreeCAD), laser engraving, acrylic joining, sewing, woodworking (e.g. slotted wooden wall).
- Building a dedicated **electronics workstation / home research lab** — multimeters, oscilloscope; analyzes components (TVS/Zener diodes, transistors like 2N5550/SS8050, mercury switches).
- Practical builds, notably a **cat-litterbox ventilation + filtration enclosure** — the same system the esp32 **cat_vent** firmware controls (see [[projects.md]]).

## Interest
- **Gaming:** Minecraft (technical minecraft), Genshin Impact (mechanics, character builds), League of Legends (Esports), Valorant (Esports).
- **learning to draw
- **Physics: major in but not graduated from.

## Pets
- Cat named **花生 (Peanut)**, shared with family. The cat_vent project exists for Peanut's litterbox.

# Projects

Most live under `~/nand2tetris_volumn/` (persistent /dev/sdc disk; survives container rebuilds). Root `/` is an ephemeral overlay — tooling installed there is lost on rebuild. Who the user is / how to collaborate: [[userprofile.md]].

## Active
- **ESP32 cat ventilation controller** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/projects/esp32/cat_vent/`) — ESP-IDF v6.0.1 firmware for the **cat-litterbox ventilation + filtration enclosure** (a physical maker build; the cat is 花生/Peanut — see [[aboutme.md]]). Current main.c is the IDF hello-world stub. Toolchain/clangd setup in [[project-context]] and [[languages.md]]. `project_setup.sh` must be **sourced** not executed.
- **Local RAG over textbooks** (planned, not built) — nomic-embed-text via Ollama + LanceDB + rank-bm25 hybrid search (60% dense / 40% BM25), OCR fallback (pytesseract / marker-pdf), exposed to Claude Code as an **MCP tool over stdio** inside the existing devcontainer. Textbooks bind-mounted read-only; rag_db as a named Docker volume. Ollama not yet installed in-container.
- **ThreePiecesSystem / OSTEP homework** (`~/nand2tetris_volumn/ThreePiecesSystem/`) — concurrency exercises. syncList.c (custom RangeLock) + approximate counter done; hand-over-hand list and B-tree locking next. See that project's own MEMORY.md.

## Hardware / maker (physical, ongoing)
- **Electronics workstation / home research lab** — building out bench tooling (multimeters, oscilloscope), component-level work. Context in [[aboutme.md]].
- Fabrication: 3D modeling (Fusion 360/FreeCAD), laser engraving, acrylic joining, woodworking. The cat_vent enclosure is the firmware↔fabrication crossover.

## Dev-environment / infra (cross-cutting)
- Containerization, dotfile management, env portability — the big ongoing meta-project. Architecture, runtime topology, migration goals in per-project memory [[dotfile-env-setup]]. Headline goals: vim→nvim, per-project containers, Dockerfile-based portability, bake ESP-IDF into an image instead of the ephemeral layer.

## Other / dormant
- `leetcodeChecklist` — node/JS project (leetcode tracking).
- `syncReaction` — youtube-bookmarker / picture-in-picture experiments.
- `champion_contest_scrapper.py` — scraper script.
- `~/LLM/` — openwebui_pipline, nginx_serve, vim_wrapper (LLM-serving experiments).
- Planned tooling: treesitter-based editing tools, vim extensions (see [[languages.md]]).

## Claude Code memory system itself
Designing the global memory tiers (Documents/Secondary/Primary/Archive) — see [[claude.md]] and todo.

# Languages, toolchains & domain knowledge

Works across embedded/C/asm, Go, Python, JS/TS, shell. Builds tools from source; comfortable at the toolchain level. Depth varies by domain — see [[userprofile.md]] (deep in C/systems; learning Go/embedded/tooling).

## C / C++ (strongest, most-discussed language)
- Systems-level: memory management, performance, concurrency, compiler behavior/optimization effects. Default to deep treatment.
- Personal lib at `~/nand2tetris_volumn/myLib` exposed via `VERITY_LIBRARY_PATH` → `CPATH`/`LIBRARY_PATH`/`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` (see [[shell.md]]). `export_Clib` moves a lib into it + symlinks back.
- LSP via clangd (coc-clangd + clangd-lsp plugin). Lint via ALE gcc+clang, format clang-format.
- Debugging: gdb+pwndbg, valgrind/helgrind. Disasm: `asmcat`.
- **ESP32 / ESP-IDF v6.0.1**: clangd needs `compile_commands.json` from `idf.py -B build.clang reconfigure` (NO `-D IDF_TOOLCHAIN=clang` — system clang lacks the Xtensa backend). Activate IDF with `source ~/.espressif/tools/activate_idf_v6.0.1.sh`. Detail in per-project memory [[project-context]].

## Go (learning, for backend dev)
- `GOPATH=~/go`, `GOBIN=~/go/bin`, go at `/usr/local/go/bin`.
- vim: vim-go + coc-go; ftplugin/go.vim uses noexpandtab, vim-go text objects (af/if/ac/ic). Lint: golangci-lint.
- Interest: concurrency primitives, practical software construction, architecture decisions.

## Python
- `python=python3` alias; ipython as default REPL (`py`/`ipy`). `PYTHONBREAKPOINT` → IPython ipdb.
- Indent = 4 spaces (vim). LSP coc-pyright, lint flake8.
- Python 3.12.13 (a source tree also sits under esp32/ for IDF).

## JS / TS
- nvm-managed node. LSP coc-tsserver. Indent 2. (leetcodeChecklist is a node project.)

## Compilers / parsers / editor tooling (interest area)
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, **treesitter**, vim plugin development. Wants to build treesitter-based editing tools and vim extensions. → [[vim.md]], [[subjects.md]]

## CS foundation
- Completed **NAND2Tetris**; implemented the **Hack VM**. Frames questions from first principles, connecting abstractions to implementation across the full system stack. Learning-domain detail in [[subjects.md]].

# Subjects discussed (running index)

Broad categories of topics covered across sessions, so past ground can be picked back up. Detail lives in the linked docs or per-project memory. Working style for all of these: [[userprofile.md]].

## OS / systems (core expertise)
- xv6 internals; Linux-inspired OS concepts. Interrupt handling, IDT/trap processing, timer interrupts, sleep/wakeup, futexes, scheduling, virtual memory.
- Recurring pattern: trace high-level API semantics down to kernel implementation; compare educational OS designs with production systems and find where simplifications break.

## Concurrency / synchronization (core expertise)
- Range locking, fine-grained vs hand-over-hand vs coarse locking, lock contention, work stealing, job dispatching, concurrent data structures.
- Themes: scalable designs, avoiding linear scans, partitioning work, preserving locality, complexity-vs-scalability tradeoffs; skeptical of textbook approaches under real workloads. → ThreePiecesSystem MEMORY.md

## Data structures (performance lens)
- Linked structures, trees, B-trees, heaps, indexing, interval/range management. Focus on why a structure exists, what gets faster/slower, behavior under insert/delete.

## Compilers / PLs / tooling internals
- Compiler & parser design, language servers, treesitter, vim plugin development, static analysis, code navigation. Strong interest in how tools work internally, wants to build vim/treesitter tooling. → [[vim.md]], [[languages.md]]
- nand2tetris stack (HDL → asm → VM → compiler → OS); Hack VM implemented.

## Embedded / hardware
- ESP32 + ESP-IDF toolchain, clangd/compile_commands, Xtensa vs system clang. → [[languages.md]], [[project-context]]
- WSL2 USB passthrough (usbipd-win) for flashing. → [[dotfile-env-setup]]
- Electronics: circuit design, hardware debugging, component analysis (TVS/Zener diodes, transistors, mercury switches), workstation/lab build. → [[aboutme.md]]

## Dev environment / infra
- Docker devcontainer topology, ephemeral vs persistent volumes, bare-repo dotfiles, declarative vs imperative provisioning, portability; vim → nvim migration. → [[dotfile-env-setup]], [[vim.md]]

## Claude Code / LLM tooling
- Claude Code extensibility (commands, skills, subagents, hooks, MCP). → [[claude.md]]
- Memory-system design (Documents/Secondary/Primary/Archive tiers, fetch-priority footnotes).
- RAG: chunk+embed+retrieve, dense+BM25 hybrid, embeddings, eval metrics (recall over precision); no built-in vector store in Claude Code → needs an MCP server. → [[projects.md]]

## Science / physics (curiosity)
- Electromagnetism, photons, quantum mechanics, engineering. Wants mechanistic explanations connecting intuition to formal models, not pop-sci. → [[aboutme.md]]

## XR / AR
- AR/XR glasses for mobile workstations (Viture, Xreal), display FOV technicalities.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

Nothing installed yet. Three things to resolve before writing code:

1. **Ollama** — install in-container (ephemeral, lost on rebuild) or run on the host and call over HTTP? Since you want to bake ESP-IDF into an image eventually, same logic applies here: Ollama in the Dockerfile is the clean path, but for now installing it ephemerally gets us moving fast.

2. **Project location** — `~/nand2tetris_volumn/projects/rag/` to keep it on the persistent volume?

3. **MCP server entrypoint** — Python script registered in `~/.claude/settings.json` under `mcpServers`?

The fastest path to first working query: install Ollama ephemerally, pull `nomic-embed-text`, stand up a minimal MCP stdio server in Python, then wire indexing and search. Want me to start with the Ollama install, or do you have a preference on any of the three questions above?
